---
title: "Real-Time Analytics with Azure Stream Analytics"
date: "2025-08-03"
categories: [Azure, Streaming, Power BI]
description: "How we built a real-time curb occupancy pipeline using Azure Stream Analytics and streamed it to Power BI dashboards with sub-minute latency."
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---

Most analytics pipelines are batch-based: pull data nightly, transform, load, refresh. That works until your stakeholders ask "but what's happening *right now*?" Here's how we solved it at Umojo.

## The use case

We track curb occupancy in real time — sensors send events every few seconds indicating whether a curb space is occupied, blocked, or available. City operations teams need live dashboards, not hourly snapshots.

**Requirements:**
- Sub-minute dashboard latency
- Handle ~5,000 events/second at peak
- Aggregate occupancy % by zone, by block, by city
- Power BI as the visualization layer

## Why Azure Stream Analytics

We evaluated Kafka + custom consumers and Azure Event Hubs + Functions before landing on Stream Analytics (ASA). The key selling points:

1. **SQL-like query language** — our team writes a lot of T-SQL; the ASA query model was immediately familiar
2. **Native Power BI output** — ASA can push directly to a Power BI streaming dataset without any glue code
3. **Windowing functions built-in** — tumbling, hopping, and sliding windows are first-class
4. **Managed service** — no cluster to maintain

## The pipeline

```
IoT Sensors
    │
    ▼
Azure Event Hub  (ingestion)
    │
    ▼
Azure Stream Analytics  (transformation)
    │
    ├──► Power BI Streaming Dataset  (live dashboard)
    │
    └──► Azure Data Lake / Delta  (historical archive)
```

## The ASA query

The core transformation aggregates sensor events into 1-minute tumbling windows:

```sql
SELECT
    Zone,
    Block,
    CityID,
    COUNT(*) AS TotalSpaces,
    SUM(CASE WHEN Status = 'Occupied' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS OccupiedSpaces,
    CAST(
        SUM(CASE WHEN Status = 'Occupied' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS FLOAT
    ) / COUNT(*) * 100 AS OccupancyPct,
    System.Timestamp() AS WindowEnd
INTO [PowerBIOutput]
FROM [EventHubInput] TIMESTAMP BY EventTime
GROUP BY
    Zone, Block, CityID,
    TumblingWindow(minute, 1)
```

One window, four output metrics, feeding live into Power BI.

## Power BI streaming dataset

The ASA output targets a Power BI streaming dataset — a special dataset type that accepts REST push and doesn't persist historical data by default (you can enable historic analysis to keep a rolling window).

In the Power BI report, tiles connected to streaming datasets update automatically without manual refresh. The dashboard latency we achieved: **18–35 seconds end-to-end** from sensor event to dashboard update.

## Handling late-arriving events

Sensors occasionally batch-send events due to network interruptions. ASA has native late-arrival tolerance:

```
Late arrival tolerance: 5 minutes
Out-of-order tolerance: 30 seconds
```

This prevents dropped events while keeping the window calculation accurate.

## Cost considerations

ASA is billed per Streaming Unit (SU). We run 3 SUs for our peak load, which handles ~5,000 events/second comfortably. Right-size before going to production — over-provisioning SUs is the most common cost mistake I've seen.

---

The full stack — Event Hub → Stream Analytics → Power BI — took about two weeks to build and tune. The operational simplicity has been worth it. If you're building something similar, [I'm happy to chat](/contact.html).
